The top card of the pack cannot count toward your opening meld requirements.
strategy: You CANNOT draw a card from stock AND pick the discard pile on the same turn. It is either/or. To pick the pack for initial meld: (1) You must already have 125+ points AND a clean triple in your hand WITHOUT any cards from the pack, (2) Lay down your meld from hand, (3) Pick up the pack, (4) Add pack cards to existing melds, (5) Discard. The top card of the pack cannot count toward your opening meld requirements.

author: Marilyn Gustman
question: Can a dead card be part of a pairs hand??
context: Whether dead cards (ranks where a canasta has been closed) can be used in special hands like pairs

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: Yes. You can use a dead card in any special hand. It can only NOT be used to meld new melds to the table once your opponent has closed the canasta.

responses.author: Kathie Wheatman
responses.text: Question for a special hand can you have four of a kind like four kings to be a pair? Kk kk

recommendation: Yes, dead cards CAN be used in special hands (pairs, garbage, etc.). Dead cards are only restricted from being melded to the table as new melds. Special hands are played from your hand directly and bypass normal melding rules.
strategy: Yes, dead cards CAN be used in special hands (pairs, garbage, etc.). Dead cards are only restricted from being melded to the table as new melds. Special hands are played from your hand directly and bypass normal melding rules.

author: Debra Asher-Zitrin
question: A player accidentally placed two aces on the table. She knew her partner had picked up a VERY dirty pack with 5 aces. The player took back her aces (illegal move). Then her partner placed 5 aces down. Can the partner meld aces after the illegal move revealed ace information?
context: Illegal meld reveals information about aces/sevens/wilds - can partner then play those cards?

responses.author: JoAnne Marshall
responses.text: It is not allowed for aces sevens or wilds if your partner lays down cards in error and you know what cards they have.

responses.author: Penny Goldner Reich
responses.text: Not allowed. Once the player had to take back her two aces, her partner CANNOT now go down with aces. Same for sevens and wilds.

recommendation: If a player makes an illegal meld revealing aces, sevens, or wilds, their PARTNER cannot then meld those same card types for the rest of that round. This prevents gaining strategic advantage from an illegal move. The rule applies specifically to aces, sevens, and wilds because these are the most strategically sensitive cards.
strategy: If a player makes an illegal meld revealing aces, sevens, or wilds, their PARTNER cannot then meld those same card types for the rest of that round. This prevents gaining strategic advantage from an illegal move. The rule applies specifically to aces, sevens, and wilds because these are the most strategically sensitive cards.

author: Susan T Williamson
question: I want to open with 5 wild cards. Do I still need a triple?
context: Can you open meld with only wild cards (no natural triple)?

responses.author: Mara Barry
responses.text: As long as they add up to the correct points needed to meld you are good.

responses.author: Frank Destefano
responses.text: If the five wild cards meet the required meld, you do not need an assist. I had six wild cards yesterday. They were all twos. I was five points short of a 125 meld. I had to put down three other cards of the same value.

responses.author: Bonnie Ashley
responses.text: Depends on where you are in melding. Can't do it with 5 2's

responses.author: Debbie Conklin-Boyd
responses.text: if it meets the required minimum points- then no

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: As long as they total the amount you need to melt, and the answer is no.

responses.author: Tanya Wolff Deutsch
responses.text: No. A wild card meld is considered clean

responses.author: Sari Zimbler Smith
responses.text: If you have the count then you can

responses.author: Carolyn Schulman
responses.text: No as long as the points are correct

responses.author: Wendy Swartz
responses.text: If you have enough points

recommendation: You do NOT need a natural triple to open with wild cards. A wild card meld counts as a clean meld. As long as the total point value of your wilds meets the opening requirement, you can meld them without any naturals. Key: 4 jokers and 1 Two = 220 pts (meets any requirement). 5 twos = only 100 pts (not enough for 125). Mix of jokers and twos may be needed to hit the point threshold.
strategy: You do NOT need a natural triple to open with wild cards. A wild card meld counts as a clean meld. As long as the total point value of your wilds meets the opening requirement, you can meld them without any naturals. Key: 4 jokers and 1 Two = 220 pts (meets any requirement). 5 twos = only 100 pts (not enough for 125). Mix of jokers and twos may be needed to hit the point threshold.

author: Sharon Smj
question: If the opposing team does not meld and each player is left with three aces, do they get 1500 for both of them or is it 1500 apiece for each of them total 3000 minus?
context: Question about whether ace penalties are per-player or per-team when both partners hold 3 aces

responses.author: Gail Nusbaum Schwartz
responses.text: 1500 each. Total minus 3,000

responses.author: Claire Parsons Riley
responses.text: Agree totally is 3000. Also, I know it may be a typo, but the term is meld not melt.

responses.author: Randi Rosensweig
responses.text: 1500 each

responses.author: Alix Kane
responses.text: Minus 1500 each player.

responses.author: Amy Elberger Meisler
responses.text: Each are minus 1500 points.

responses.author: Rhea Abelson Feinberg
responses.text: At the end partners combine their cards and deduct total. So 6 aces in hand still amount to 1500.

responses.author: Marcia Sternlieb
responses.text: 1500 each is deducted

responses.author: Fran Karliner
responses.text: Minus 3,000

responses.author: Estel Punsky Silverstein
responses.text: 1500 minus for each

recommendation: Each player's hand is scored individually. If both partners each hold 3 aces, that's 1500 penalty PER PLAYER, totaling 3000 for the team. Hands are not combined before calculating ace penalties.
complexity_note: One minority view says partners combine cards first (6 aces = 1500), but overwhelming consensus is per-player scoring
strategy: Each player's hand is scored individually. If both partners each hold 3 aces, that's 1500 penalty PER PLAYER, totaling 3000 for the team. Hands are not combined before calculating ace penalties.

author: Robin Wiplich Williams
question: If i picked the pack (1 card) and want to go out, can I use a 7 (my last card) as the discard??
context: Question about final face-down discard when going out after picking the pack.

responses.author: April Volponi
responses.text: Yes -- when you go out, the final card is face down, so it may be any card (not a 3)

responses.author: Jill Klaskin Press
responses.text: It can be a 3 in our game… a down card is a down card

responses.author: Dale Freedman Pilchman
responses.text: Why not

responses.author: Beverly Chassen Resnick
responses.text: Yes, if is face down, why not a 3?

recommendation: Yes — you can discard a 7 as your final face-down discard when going out after picking the pack. Clear recommendation: use the 7. On the 'can it be a 3?' debate, treat that as table-specific unless your group has explicitly agreed.
complexity_note: Less-complexity rule applied: clear recommendation given despite mixed table-rule comments.
strategy: Yes — you can discard a 7 as your final face-down discard when going out after picking the pack. Clear recommendation: use the 7. On the 'can it be a 3?' debate, treat that as table-specific unless your group has explicitly agreed.

author: Christine Casha DeStefanis
question: If you pick a three and it's the last card in the deck so there's no option to pick another card, do you have to discard?
context: End-of-deck edge case involving drawing a three as the final stock card.

responses.author: Kim Schermer Fetterman
responses.text: No. The three stays in your hand. It is only deducted for five points and the game is over.

responses.author: Jim Weiss
responses.text: This is governed by a table rule. Some play it stays in your hand and -5 points. Others play you must put it down.

recommendation: Clear recommendation (less complexity): do NOT require an extra discard. Keep the drawn three in hand, apply the 5-point penalty, and end the hand.
complexity_note: Community notes this can be a table-rule variation, but no-extra-action handling is simpler and avoids conditional exceptions.
strategy: Clear recommendation (less complexity): do NOT require an extra discard. Keep the drawn three in hand, apply the 5-point penalty, and end the hand.

author: Marilyn Gustman
question: Can you open with a wild card canasta and at the same time use a wild card with a pair in order to get the points needed?
context: Whether you can simultaneously start a wild card canasta AND use a wild card in another meld during your opening meld.

responses.author: April Volponi
responses.text: No. All cards must go to the wild card canasta until it is closed. Then you may add other wild cards to other melds.

responses.author: Andrea Karshan Stein
responses.text: Are you saying it's literally a closed canasta? If so it's enough to open & you don't need anything else. If it's 5 or 6 then it may or may not be enough

responses.author: Kathy Weisman Wolf
responses.text: Absolutely not. If you open with a wild card canasta you can never use a wild card on anything else until that wild card canasta is closed.

responses.author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
responses.text: No! Once you put down the wilds you must complete it before adding onto other cards.

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: No!!!! If you open with a wild card meld you would have to close that canasta before you can use wilds with anything else.

responses.author: Shelley Wohl Minsky
responses.text: Of course not

responses.author: Mario Cavalcanti
responses.text: No…

responses.author: Marla Matthias
responses.text: No!

responses.author: Rose Marie
responses.text: No

recommendation: You can, but it is not good strategy. Not being able to close a wild card canasta is a very costly penalty.  Using wild cards for other melds makes it harder for you to close your wild card canasta and the risk is not worth the opening opportunity.
strategy: You can, but it is not good strategy. Not being able to close a wild card canasta is a very costly penalty.  Using wild cards for other melds makes it harder for you to close your wild card canasta and the risk is not worth the opening opportunity.

author: Wendy Tauber Grecco
question: The person before me throws an ace. I have enough points to lay down my opening meld without the ace. Can I pick the pack with the ace on top and add it to my opening meld of dirty aces?
context: Whether you can pick the pack with an ace to open with a dirty/mixed ace meld.

responses.author: Meryl Danitz
responses.text: No. If you pick the pack with an ace on top you will have to open it as a pure aces canasta. Once you are already open, if you opened with a dirty aces meld, you or your partner can then pick the pack if an ace is thrown and you have a pair of aces in your hand, and add that to your dirty aces meld.

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: No. Unless dirty Aces is already melded if you pick up Aces you have to make a clean canasta.

responses.author: Kathy Weisman Wolf
responses.text: If you pick the pack with an Ace you must meld them clean!

responses.author: Barbara Magliozzi
responses.text: You would need 2 in hand to pick it up.

responses.author: Rose Marie
responses.text: Yes as long as you don't take a card, meld with the correct points and you are using the pair of Aces you have on the table.

responses.author: Shelley Wohl Minsky
responses.text: Absolutely not

responses.author: Donna Miller-Small
responses.text: When picking the pack to Open with an Ace as the top card, the Ace Meld must be PURE, not MIXED.

responses.author: Debbie Conklin-Boyd
responses.text: No

responses.author: Ella Hollander Modell
responses.text: No, they would have to be natural.

responses.author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
responses.text: No, you can not pick the pack for dirty Aces when you meld.

recommendation: As long as you finish your opening meld with your dirty aces and another natural meld and you have your point requirements without picking a card from the stock, you can pick the ace and the discard pile and add it to your hand.
strategy: As long as you finish your opening meld with your dirty aces and another natural meld and you have your point requirements without picking a card from the stock, you can pick the ace and the discard pile and add it to your hand.

author: Sharon Strauss Roth
question: When you have met all the requirements, is it a table rule or MAC rule that allows a player to go out with two cards and a wild card?
context: Whether going out with only a pair + wild card (3-card meld) is allowed under standard MAC rules or is a table/house rule.

responses.author: Andi Stone Katz
responses.text: If you play a rule of 5, then no, you can't go out with a pair and a wild. Yes, you can go out when you play a rule of 3.

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: It's a table rule, some do two cards while, three cards just decide which you prefer. Most people do two cards and a wild, that's the way I teach and play.

responses.author: Barbara Magliozzi
responses.text: Table rule. Many online don't allow it. Base is always a table rule. Normal is 5 and only to go out can you put 2 with a wild, some would never allow it.

responses.author: Modern American Canasta
responses.text: The Meld Base should stay consistent throughout the round. The most widely played rule does not allow you to end the round this way. We believe this crept in as a House Rule based on speeding up tournament rounds.

responses.author: Shelley Wohl Minsky
responses.text: Table rule and if you play meld 5 you cannot go out with 2 and a wild.

responses.author: Penny Goldner Reich
responses.text: We play a meld base of 5. We do not go out with [2+wild].

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: We play rule of 5 so this would not be allowed.

responses.author: Jodi Lieberman Most
responses.text: Table Rule. Not allowed in our game.

recommendation: Going out with just 2 cards + a wild card is a TABLE RULE, not standard MAC. The MAC admin account confirms the standard rule requires the meld base to stay consistent — if you play rule-of-5 (most common), all melds including the final one need 5 cards minimum. Clear recommendation: do NOT allow going out with 2+wild unless your table has explicitly agreed to allow it.
complexity_note: Less-complexity rule applied: clear recommendation despite some tables allowing it.
strategy: Going out with just 2 cards + a wild card is a TABLE RULE, not standard MAC. The MAC admin account confirms the standard rule requires the meld base to stay consistent — if you play rule-of-5 (most common), all melds including the final one need 5 cards minimum. Clear recommendation: do NOT allow going out with 2+wild unless your table has explicitly agreed to allow it.

author: Susan Scholar
question: One partner goes down with open wilds. On the next turn her partner picks the card for a special hand. Does the open wild card canasta count against them?
context: Whether an incomplete wild card canasta penalizes the team when the partner goes out with a special hand (Courtesy Special).

responses.author: Chris Bradley
responses.text: Most groups I play with say that a special hand wipes out opponents score. So nothing counts either positively or negatively.

responses.author: April Volponi
responses.text: No penalty -- it's called a Courtesy Special. You score a flat-rate for the points for the special hand -- no impact regarding open wilds.

recommendation: When a partner goes out with a special hand (Courtesy Special), an open/incomplete wild card canasta does NOT count against the team. The special hand scores a flat rate — no penalties or bonuses from melds on the table apply.
strategy: When a partner goes out with a special hand (Courtesy Special), an open/incomplete wild card canasta does NOT count against the team. The special hand scores a flat rate — no penalties or bonuses from melds on the table apply.

author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
question: My partner melds with 125 and has one joker in her meld. I have 4 jokers in my hand. I proceed to lay them down hoping to make wilds — so I am gathering, is it allowed until I complete my wilds?
context: Can you start a wild card canasta after your partner already used a wild in another meld?

responses.author: Carole Giacalone Sferrazza
responses.text: Cannot go out with open wilds even if you have two canastas. Should never open with wilds after partner melded with a wild card.

responses.author: Beth F. Levine
responses.text: Strategy wise, I was taught that you never lay down aces sevens or wilds less than five and only if you've been watching what the other team has.

recommendation: You CAN start a wild card canasta after your partner used a wild in another meld. However, strategy-wise: never start a wild card canasta with fewer than 5 wilds, and only if you've been tracking what the opponents hold. Starting with just 4 is risky — you may get stuck with open wilds and be unable to go out.
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: clarified what's legal vs. what's strategic.
strategy: You CAN start a wild card canasta after your partner used a wild in another meld. However, strategy-wise: never start a wild card canasta with fewer than 5 wilds, and only if you've been tracking what the opponents hold. Starting with just 4 is risky — you may get stuck with open wilds and be unable to go out.

author: Patti Heleva
question: Team A melds with a wild card Canasta (only Canasta they have). Team B goes out with a special hand. Can Team A count that 1 wild card Canasta towards their base for 2000 pts or do you have to have 2 Canastas?
context: Whether a single wild card canasta counts toward the 2000-point base bonus when the opposing team goes out with a special hand.

responses.author: Sterra Himelstein
responses.text: Wild canasta counts. You don't have to have 2 canastas. Only necessary for adding in the 3s. Of course, they'd need 2 canastas to go out. But it counts.

responses.author: Robin Levine
responses.text: When one team gets a Special Hand the other team is scored as if the round ended regularly. Therefore, they get the full value of their Wild Card Canasta and all their meld including the value of the individual wild cards and any other meld on the table. They don't get their 3s.

responses.author: Debbie Meyer
responses.text: Unless you play wipeout, then the wildcard canasta doesn't count or any of their points as the special hand would wipe everything out.

recommendation: Yes, a single wild card canasta counts toward the 2000-point canasta base. When the opposing team goes out with a special hand, your team is scored as if the round ended normally — you get full credit for all melds on the table, including your wild card canasta.
strategy: Yes, a single wild card canasta counts toward the 2000-point canasta base. When the opposing team goes out with a special hand, your team is scored as if the round ended normally — you get full credit for all melds on the table, including your wild card canasta.

author: Gillian Reichman
question: If you have four queens in your hand and a joker, are you allowed to meld the queens and discard the joker to go out? This is for the five card rule.
context: Under the 5-card meld base rule, can you meld 4 naturals without a wild and discard the wild to go out?

responses.author: Amy Cooper Zenn
responses.text: Yes. Can discard any card to go out as long as you have 2 closed canastas

responses.author: Barbara Radin Katzeff
responses.text: Yes, we play you only need two canastas to go out

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: Yes, provided you have 2 closed canastas. Wild goes face down to go out.

responses.author: Amy Elberger Meisler
responses.text: In our game you may discard any card you like since that card is always thrown face down.

responses.author: Jamie Lewin Meadow
responses.text: The only card you may not discard is a three

responses.author: Judy Blavin
responses.text: You can discard a joker to go out but you have been better off if you put down three queens & the wild — you could then discard a queen (more points).

responses.author: RoseAnn Pilo Silecchia
responses.text: If it's a 5 card table rule I don't think you can put down 4 queens

responses.author: Kathy Rollin
responses.text: I don't believe so. You need to discard the queen since you have the option and hold on to your joker.

responses.author: Judy Blavin
responses.text: We play 3 cards & if you go out, you can put two cards & a wild out. So many different table rules!

responses.author: Jim Weiss
responses.text: Having read all of the responses, the answer is unquestionably either YES or NO, depends who you ask!

recommendation: Under the 5-card meld base rule, you CANNOT meld only 4 queens — the meld base of 5 means every new meld needs at least 5 cards. However, if you're going out and adding to an EXISTING queens meld on the table, that's fine. The real question is about the discard: yes, you can discard any card to go out (including a joker/wild) as long as you have your 2 required canastas.: it's better to meld 3 queens + the wild (4 cards added to existing meld or as part of going out) and discard a queen — you score more points that way.
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: community split on whether 4 naturals alone satisfies rule-of-5. Clear recommendation: under rule-of-5, new melds need 5 cards minimum, so 4 queens alone is not a valid new meld.
strategy: Under the 5-card meld base rule, you CANNOT meld only 4 queens — the meld base of 5 means every new meld needs at least 5 cards. However, if you're going out and adding to an EXISTING queens meld on the table, that's fine. The real question is about the discard: yes, you can discard any card to go out (including a joker/wild) as long as you have your 2 required canastas.: it's better to meld 3 queens + the wild (4 cards added to existing meld or as part of going out) and discard a queen — you score more points that way.

author: Mary Fiore
question: If I need 180 to meld, can I come down with five aces and two wild cards without a clean if it satisfies my 180 meld?
context: Whether you can open/meld with only dirty melds (naturals + wilds) if the point total meets the meld requirement, without including a clean triple.

responses.author: Chris Bradley
responses.text: No. You cannot open with a dirty canasta. Clean canasta of any card allows you to open. In other words, a clean canasta of 5's works even if it doesn't total 180

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: Only if you gave a splash - 7 of the same card satisfies that meld whatever it might be. 125,155,180 As stated above still need clean triple as well.

responses.author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
responses.text: Yes- but you must have a triple clean with it to meld- even though you made 180 with your Aces and jokers

responses.author: Rita Hirshman Trokenheim
responses.text: No you need a clean triple

responses.author: Debbie Meyer
responses.text: Must have a clean trip.

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: No. You would need a clean triple.

responses.author: Sheryl Silberman
responses.text: No. You need a pure

responses.author: Robin Hammer
responses.text: No

responses.author: Leslie Schneider Weiner
responses.text: No

responses.author: Penny Goldner Reich
responses.text: Yes

recommendation: You CANNOT meld with only dirty melds, even if the point total meets the requirement. You MUST include a clean triple (3+ natural cards of the same rank, no wilds) in your initial meld. The only exception is a 'splash' — a complete clean canasta (7 of the same natural card), which satisfies any meld requirement regardless of its point value. Meeting the point threshold alone is not enough; the clean triple requirement is separate and mandatory.
strategy: You CANNOT meld with only dirty melds, even if the point total meets the requirement. You MUST include a clean triple (3+ natural cards of the same rank, no wilds) in your initial meld. The only exception is a 'splash' — a complete clean canasta (7 of the same natural card), which satisfies any meld requirement regardless of its point value. Meeting the point threshold alone is not enough; the clean triple requirement is separate and mandatory.

author: Sue Kline
question: If you are waiting for one card to make a special hand, and right hand opponent throws that card into an empty tray, can you pick the card up to make the special hand?
context: Whether you can complete a special hand by picking up a single card from the discard pile instead of drawing from the stock pile.

responses.author: Ruth Mezistrano
responses.text: Online canasta lets you but most games you have to pick it yourself from the deck.

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: Once again a TABLE RULE.. doing that way has no strategy, no way for you to learn, build your game up, switch hand around. Way too many tables rules to make game way to easy to play! Try picking it yourself!!

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: No. Special hands have to come from the regular pile not the discard pile. !!!

responses.author: Barbara Magliozzi
responses.text: It is a table rule and only if there is 1 card.

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: No. The card must be picked from the pile, not the discard, to complete a special hand

responses.author: Sharon Gillman Leff
responses.text: My group says you can and I've been in many tournaments and they let you

responses.author: Beverly Chassen Resnick
responses.text: No. It has to be picked from the deck

responses.author: Ruthanne Donahue Switzer
responses.text: Maybe a table rule???

responses.author: Sondra Berman
responses.text: Yes

responses.author: Sheree Glickman-Mancuso
responses.text: Yes but it's a table rule.

recommendation: Special hands must be completed from the deck and not the discard pile unless your table has explicitly agreed otherwise.
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: community split but standard rule is clear — must draw from deck.
strategy: Special hands must be completed from the deck and not the discard pile unless your table has explicitly agreed otherwise.

author: Lydia Weiselberg
question: Partner had 4 aces, 5 deuces, and 1 joker. Needed 180 to meld. Can she put down 5 deuces and then put down 4 aces with the joker to meld?
context: Whether you can split wild cards between a wild card canasta and a dirty natural meld when opening — specifically, putting down 5 deuces as start of wild canasta, then using the joker with 4 aces as a separate dirty meld.

responses.author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
responses.text: No, once she put down the 5 deuces she was obliged to complete it - that joker had to go with the deuces

responses.author: Donna Bottone Bianco
responses.text: No. The wild cards all need to be put down together

responses.author: Sheryl Frenkel Gordon
responses.text: No. You need to put one clean meld down to open

responses.author: Abigail Urban Rifkin
responses.text: No. You would have to use the aces as your clean triple and go for a joker canasta. Your aces would now have to be pure.

responses.author: Marleen Leventhal
responses.text: Absolutely NOT. Once you begin a wildcard Canasta all of your wild cards MUST be placed on the wild cards to complete that canasta

responses.author: Rebecca Salame Harary
responses.text: Nope. Can't do that until the joker canasta is closed. Then if there are extra jokers, you can add them to other melds. HOWEVER, once aces are on the table as clean, you CANNOT add a joker to it. It must be closed as a clean canasta, which is worth 2500 points.

responses.author: Frank Destefano
responses.text: No but I would have taken the risk. Your job is to pick up the pack and pray

responses.author: Kathleen Miguel
responses.text: No.

responses.author: Barbara Magliozzi
responses.text: I would have gone for clean A's at that point if there were none thrown nor out.

responses.author: Penny Goldner Reich
responses.text: No. You cannot use a wild card anywhere until you have closed wilds.

recommendation: This is simply not good strategy. Many tables say you cannot split your wilds on opening between a wild canasta and a dirty meld. We don't see it as a rule, but as a bad strategy.  Getting a wild card canasta is hard enough.  Using your wilds for another meld only makes it more difficult to complete the wilds canasta, which would carry a 2000 point penalty if the round ends and you don't close it.
strategy: This is simply not good strategy. Many tables say you cannot split your wilds on opening between a wild canasta and a dirty meld. We don't see it as a rule, but as a bad strategy.  Getting a wild card canasta is hard enough.  Using your wilds for another meld only makes it more difficult to complete the wilds canasta, which would carry a 2000 point penalty if the round ends and you don't close it.

author: Michelle Hodes Ehrman
question: If you're playing the 5 card rule, when you're going out only, isn't it allowed to put down 2 cards and a joker?
context: Under the 5-card meld base rule, can you put down fewer than 5 cards (e.g. 2 naturals + 1 wild = 3 cards) as a new meld when going out?

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: You can only use 2 cards and a joker when opening. If you are going out in the rule of 5 you would need 5 matching cards to add the wild.

responses.author: Sharon Bosacki
responses.text: Yes. You have to also still have 3 naturals to lay down after an initial meld. And then 5 naturals to add any wild cards to that meld.

responses.author: Alix Kane
responses.text: If you're playing a 5-card base, a pair and a wild card is only fine when you meld and when you go out. Not during the game

responses.author: Alix Kane
responses.text: I never thought it was a table rule.

responses.author: Joy Levine Zelin
responses.text: No

responses.author: Bonnie Glick Witler
responses.text: table rule

responses.author: Karen Waters Waas
responses.text: 100% a table rule! Have fun!

responses.author: Amy Elberger Meisler
responses.text: Depends on your table rules.

responses.author: Robin Levine
responses.text: This is how you can go out in Base 5

responses.author: Arlene Aruta
responses.text: Yes

responses.author: Barbara Goichman
responses.text: No

recommendation: when going out you can put down 2 cards and a joker. Once your team has gone out, you can only put a wild after you have 5 natural cards.
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: community evenly split. Clear recommendation given: default to requiring 5 cards even when going out, but acknowledge the going-out exception as a widespread table rule that should be agreed upon before play.
strategy: when going out you can put down 2 cards and a joker. Once your team has gone out, you can only put a wild after you have 5 natural cards.

author: Michelle Hodes Ehrman
question: Playing the 5-card rule: when going out, is it allowed to go out with 2 cards and a joker (less than 5 natural cards)?
community_answer: Split but leaning NO. Barbara Schnur: table rule, we say NO. Penny Goldner Reich (Top contributor): teaching 2+joker to go out as NO. Lori Drucker: not in my game, house rule. Marla Lantz: no, we like games challenging. Nalda Rothe: yes, only time you can use 2+joker is opening or going out. Joanne Blau Bellet: yes. Author concludes: table rule for sure.
recommendation: You can definitely go out with less than 5 natural cards, but only when going out. Once you or your partner goes out, the rule of 5 natural cards applies moving forward.
strategy: You can definitely go out with less than 5 natural cards, but only when going out. Once you or your partner goes out, the rule of 5 natural cards applies moving forward.

author: Leslie Carol Berg Weiss
question: If you open late and take your 3-card talon but opponents go out before you can replace the 3 on your next turn, how is the 3 stuck in your hand scored?
community_answer: Split. Stephanie Adler Calliott: -100 (but some play -5). Penny Goldner Reich (Top contributor): minus 5 or minus 100, thinks -5 is enough punishment since you also lose credit for that 3. Dana Ross (Rising contributor): minus 5. Missy Lans Retsky: partner should ask before going out so you can decide.
recommendation: Score the unreplaced talon 3 stuck in hand as -5 points. The player already loses the bonus credit for that 3 (provided they closed 2 canastas), which is penalty enough. The -100 interpretation adds unnecessary harshness for a situation largely outside the player's control.
strategy: Score the unreplaced talon 3 stuck in hand as -5 points. The player already loses the bonus credit for that 3 (provided they closed 2 canastas), which is penalty enough. The -100 interpretation adds unnecessary harshness for a situation largely outside the player's control.

author: Jane Veksler
question: When you get a 3, you take it, then replace it with another card and then you get another card?
context: Clarification on the procedure for drawing a red 3 during play — how many replacement cards you draw.

responses.author: Judy Eller
responses.text: When you draw a 3 you put it down on the table and then draw another card to replace it. If you draw another 3 continue putting on the table and replace it by drawing another card till it anything but a 3 then meld or discard. If you pick a 3 in your talon, hold till your next turn. If you forget to place the 3 on the table after your turn, you hold it till end of game and take a penalty of 5 points.

responses.author: Robin Levine
responses.text: If you are dealt a 3 when it is your turn you meld the 3 on the table and take 1 for me and 1 for the 3. If you get a 3 in your Talon you get a second time to take a 3 and 1 for me.

responses.author: Donna Bottone Bianco
responses.text: Yes. 1 card for your turn and 1 card for each 3

responses.author: Susan Silberstein
responses.text: Mandatory to meld threes unless going for a straight then if get second wild MUST meld it no replacement not a table rule

responses.author: Debbie Meyer
responses.text: Correct; you get one card for your turn and one card for your 3.

responses.author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
responses.text: When you pick a 3 you replace it and then you pick another card for yourself

responses.author: Barbara Cooper
responses.text: Yes.

recommendation: When you draw a red 3 from the stock pile, place it face-up on the table immediately and draw one replacement card. You still get your normal draw for the turn. If you draw multiple 3s in a row, keep placing on the table and replacing until you draw a non-3. If you draw a 3 from your talon, hold it until your next turn (since you already discarded).
strategy: When you draw a red 3 from the stock pile, place it face-up on the table immediately and draw one replacement card. You still get your normal draw for the turn. If you draw multiple 3s in a row, keep placing on the table and replacing until you draw a non-3. If you draw a 3 from your talon, hold it until your next turn (since you already discarded).

author: Heidi Sue
question: Special hands scoring reference chart shared by All-star contributor
context: Heidi Sue posted an image listing special hands and their point values: Boca Pairs (with matching wilds, aces, 7s) = 2000, Pairs (no wilds) = 2500, Miami Pairs = 3000, Straight (Ace thru Joker) = 3500, Garbage Hand (4-4-3-3 no wilds) = 3000, Four 3-of-a-kind + matching wilds (3-3-3-3-2) = 3500, Three 4-of-a-kind + 2 matching wilds (4-4-4-2) = 3500, Zip Code (no wilds, 2-2-3-3-4-of-a-kind) = 2000, Blast Off (5-4-3-2 opt matching wilds) = 3000, Dream Hand (5-5-4 no wilds) = 8500.

responses.author: Lori Greene
responses.text: Zip code is 2500 with or without wilds. Miami pairs hand is 2500. Straight is 3000 not 3500. Your scoring is all table rules. Never heard pairs hand with 1 set of wilds called Boca Pairs!

responses.author: Christine Allan
responses.text: Haven't heard of many of these

responses.author: Lynda Balzer D'Alessio
responses.text: I thought the zip code was 1500? I'm sure I'm wrong.

responses.author: Robyn Bross Cohn
responses.text: Varies by table rules

responses.author: Judy Friduss Leib
responses.text: We give 2500 for everything. Easy!

recommendation: Special hand point values vary widely by table and region. There is no single authoritative scoring chart. The most common approach for simplicity is to score all special hands at 2500 points. If your table uses differentiated scoring, agree on values before play begins. Key disputes: Zip Code ranges from 1500-2500, Straight is either 3000 or 3500, Miami Pairs is either 2500 or 3000. Regional names like 'Boca Pairs' are not universally recognized.
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: huge variation in scoring. Recommendation: use flat 2500 for all special hands unless table agrees otherwise.
strategy: Special hand point values vary widely by table and region. There is no single authoritative scoring chart. The most common approach for simplicity is to score all special hands at 2500 points. If your table uses differentiated scoring, agree on values before play begins. Key disputes: Zip Code ranges from 1500-2500, Straight is either 3000 or 3500, Miami Pairs is either 2500 or 3000. Regional names like 'Boca Pairs' are not universally recognized.

author: Linda Ellis Finkelstein
question: The straight special hand is A2345678910JQK = 13 cards. If you draw from the stock pile, you'd have 15 cards. What's the correct composition?
context: Confusion about the straight special hand card count. The poster listed A through K as 13 cards, forgetting the Big Joker.

responses.author: Andi Stone Katz
responses.text: You are missing the big joker for this straight.

responses.author: Bonnie Ashley
responses.text: Big Joker is a must.

responses.author: Pat Zimble Wadleigh
responses.text: Need a joker

responses.author: Frank Destefano
responses.text: Joker in that hand makes 14

responses.author: Jill PG
responses.text: A joker as well

responses.author: Linda Gaddis
responses.text: Joker is needed

responses.author: Ellen Maurer
responses.text: The hand you are showing is called Garbage, one of a kind Plus a Big Joker

responses.author: Julie Seidenfeld Schumer
responses.text: Specials are made with 14 cards.

responses.author: Susie Malik
responses.text: For this hand you must also have the big joker

responses.author: Joanne Blau Bellet
responses.text: You forgot the Big Boy aka the Joker.

responses.author: Alysia Ehrlich Carrillo
responses.text: You're missing the big Joker in there that makes 14

responses.author: Caroline Dann
responses.text: You need your joker!

responses.author: Donna Bottone Bianco
responses.text: Big joker is needed

responses.author: Elise Efrus
responses.text: You need the big joker

responses.author: Lisa Fox
responses.text: Garbage is 4-4-3-3. This is a straight.

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: It was called garbage many many years ago! A garbage hand is now 4,4,3,3. A straight which think about it: J-K 14 cards, one of each card!

responses.author: Mary Ann Sperando
responses.text: it's also referred to as a straight

recommendation: A straight special hand consists of 14 cards: Ace through King (one of each, 13 cards) plus the Big Joker. All special hands are 14 cards total (13 from your hand + 1 drawn from the stock pile).
strategy: A straight special hand consists of 14 cards: Ace through King (one of each, 13 cards) plus the Big Joker. All special hands are 14 cards total (13 from your hand + 1 drawn from the stock pile).

author: Lydia Weiselberg
question: I melted with 180 points and two of my cards that I melded with were 2 nines. We play that we can take the pack as long as you melted with the correct number of points. Somebody else told us that in order to take that pack, we needed another two nines in our hands, not part of the melt.
context: Whether the pair of cards used to pick up the pack can be cards already in your opening meld, or if you need a separate additional pair in hand not counting toward the meld.

responses.author: Andi Stone Katz
responses.text: Table rules can make you crazy. Yes, you can pick the pack as you said, as long as your meld is correct without using the nine on the top of the stock.

responses.author: Jodi Lieberman Most
responses.text: You can take the pack using the 2 nines in your hand that you MELDED with.

responses.author: April Volponi
responses.text: Rule of thumb -- take the pack and put it to the side. Meld using only the cards in your hand. When that has been done, you can take the pack and do whatever you want with it. In other words, your 2 nines didn't count (unless you added a wild card to them) towards your opening meld of 180

responses.author: Barbara Magliozzi
responses.text: The top card plus 2 in your hand do not count toward 180. You also cant use it as clean 3. But any cards you add to first 3 you have in hand count. Example you have 4 9's in hand and initial meld want that top card, you get only 20 points towards first meld for the 2 extra 9's. But if have the other 160 and another clean triple should be good. Some tables won't allow you to use any cards from it.

responses.author: Beth F. Levine
responses.text: On an initial meld, you can pick the pack with either 2 cards on the table or 2 cards in your hand, as long as you have met the meld point requirements. All meld requirements, including your natural must be met prior to picking the pack!

responses.author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
responses.text: Was the 2-9's just two cards in your meld and you needed the 9 in the pack to make your required points? If so you can not pick up the pack. But if you had the right amount of points laid down on the table and you had the 2/9's in your hand? Yes you can pick the pack

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: As long as absolutely nothing counts towards your meld you can pick the pack. You do not need another 2 Ace's as long as you don't need it towards the meld

responses.author: Christine Allan
responses.text: We play you must meld on the table to equal 125, 155 or 180. To pick the pack you need two cards in your hand that match the top card in the pile. Cannot count towards your meld.

responses.author: Gayle Kollitz
responses.text: No. Unless that is a very unusual table rule. Your meld has to be right before adding from the pack. If your two nines did not have a wild, you could have added one nine from your hand to the meld and picked the pack.

responses.author: Rose Marie
responses.text: Put your 180 points down correctly. Like you weren't taking the pack ..then the pair in your hand OR THE PAIR IN YOUR 180 COUNT will enable you to pick up the pack and put all down you want

responses.author: Merri Geri
responses.text: I thought you needed a natural and three or more of other cards to make 180. So, two 9 can't be counted towards your 180.

responses.author: Judy Hill
responses.text: Correct

recommendation: To pick up the pack when opening: first, satisfy ALL meld requirements (point threshold + clean triple) using ONLY cards from your hand — pretend the pack doesn't exist. The top card of the discard pile and the pair you use to pick it up do NOT count toward your meld requirement. Once your meld is valid on its own, you can pick the pack with a matching pair — and that pair CAN be cards already on the table as part of your meld, OR cards still in your hand. The key rule: your meld must stand alone without any help from the pack.
strategy: To pick up the pack when opening: first, satisfy ALL meld requirements (point threshold + clean triple) using ONLY cards from your hand — pretend the pack doesn't exist. The top card of the discard pile and the pair you use to pick it up do NOT count toward your meld requirement. Once your meld is valid on its own, you can pick the pack with a matching pair — and that pair CAN be cards already on the table as part of your meld, OR cards still in your hand. The key rule: your meld must stand alone without any help from the pack.

author: Amy Beer Brown
question: What is the deduction for 3s if you have no canastas at end of round? Is there a difference for red vs black threes?

positions.view: Penny Goldner Reich: 1 three=100, 2=300, 3=500, 4=1000 per color. Same for red and black. Some say -5 or -100 for 3s still in hand.
Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky: Same 100/300/500/1000 scale per color. Each color scored separately. Making one canasta saves your 3s (no deduction).
Gail Robinson Ragusa: You also lose points for all cards in hand AND on table, in addition to 3s penalties.
Debbi Moldawer Sobel: Everything including 3s are deducted from your score.

recommendation: 3s penalty when no canastas: 100/300/500/1000 per color (red and black scored separately but same scale). Having at least one completed canasta saves your 3s from penalty. For 3s still in hand at round end, count -100 each (the more common interpretation). All melds on table also count against you when no canastas.

author: Chris Bradley
topic: Can Opponents Add to Melds After Rank is Closed (Canasta Completed)
question: If opponents had 3 fours down and I closed the fours by completing a canasta, can the opposing team add anything to their open 4s on subsequent turns?
community_position: Unanimous consensus (10+ commenters all agree: No)

positions.view: Susie Linder Rozanczyk (Top contributor): No. Once a canasta is finished the card is dead so no other canastas can be made with it. (4 reactions)
Cheryl Chesa Fosket: Definitely dead once closed. (3 reactions)
Sheryl Kleinbart Cohen: No. 4s are dead. (2 reactions)
Sharon Gillman Leff: Dead unless used in a special hand. (2 reactions)
Patricia Grainger: No. They can still be used in special hands and as discards. (1 reaction)
Paula Rozan: My answer is no, but I've seen groups declare it a yes as their table rule. (1 reaction)
Penny Goldner Reich (Top contributor): No. (1 reaction)
Chris Bradley follow-up: Can they add wild cards to the 4s? — Also No per replies.

recommendation: Once a rank is closed (canasta completed by either team), that rank is DEAD. No team can add any cards (natural or wild) to their melds of that rank. The cards of that rank can still be used in special hands or as discards. Some casual groups allow adding as a house rule, but standard Modern American Canasta rules do not.
strategy: Once a rank is closed (canasta completed by either team), that rank is DEAD. No team can add any cards (natural or wild) to their melds of that rank. The cards of that rank can still be used in special hands or as discards. Some casual groups allow adding as a house rule, but standard Modern American Canasta rules do not.

author: Helaine Tantleff Neiman
question: Do you allow opening with a closed canasta made of five aces and two wilds, plus a clean triple? A clean canasta is allowed, but what about a dirty one?
context: Whether you can open with a dirty (mixed) closed canasta — 5 naturals + 2 wilds completing 7 cards — as your opening meld, alongside a clean triple.

responses.author: Alix Kane
responses.text: If you want your closed canasta to be your meld, without having the points for meld, it must be a natural canasta. If dirty, you need the meld points.

responses.author: Robin Levine
responses.text: If you have 5 Aces plus two wilds and a clean triple that covers your meld point requirement it is absolutely fine to meld with an opening Canasta of dirty Aces plus a clean triple... a Canasta is a Canasta... anything else is a table rule.

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: I don't see why not. As long as you have a clean triple and the meld you need, go for it! One down & one to go! Good Luck

responses.author: Estel Punsky Silverstein
responses.text: Yes, as long as you have a clean triple

responses.author: Marcia Sternlieb
responses.text: Yes

responses.author: Janet Levine
responses.text: As long as you meet the point requirements

responses.author: Rita Hirshman Trokenheim
responses.text: Only if it meets the amount you need to open and you need a clean triple

responses.author: Jamie Lewin Meadow
responses.text: Nope

responses.author: Rae Glaser
responses.text: No. Has to open clean.

responses.author: Jennifer Jackson-Strage
responses.text: Never

recommendation: Yes, you CAN open with a dirty closed canasta (e.g., 5 aces + 2 wilds = 7 cards), but ONLY if you also meet the meld point requirement for your round AND include a clean triple. The key distinction: a CLEAN (all-natural) canasta bypasses the meld point requirement entirely, while a DIRTY canasta still requires meeting the point threshold. This is standard Canasta rules, not a table rule.
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: some players insist on clean-only opening canastas, but top contributors and the All-star agree dirty is fine with point requirements met.
strategy: Yes, you CAN open with a dirty closed canasta (e.g., 5 aces + 2 wilds = 7 cards), but ONLY if you also meet the meld point requirement for your round AND include a clean triple. The key distinction: a CLEAN (all-natural) canasta bypasses the meld point requirement entirely, while a DIRTY canasta still requires meeting the point threshold. This is standard Canasta rules, not a table rule.

author: Judy Levinthal Workman
question: If you meld and also pick the pile, do you get your 3 or 4 cards (talon)?
context: Whether a player who opens their meld AND picks up the discard pile also gets their talon (3 or 4 bonus cards for first/second team to meld).

responses.author: Bonnie Ashley
responses.text: You get none. The pack replaces picking.

responses.author: Robin Levine
responses.text: No, you have a choice to pick the pack and meld and get to use all the cards in the pack or to just meld and take your Talon of 4 or 3 cards depending upon whether you're the first or second melder in the round.

responses.author: Patti Heleva
responses.text: It's one or the other, not both

responses.author: Harriet Wilner
responses.text: That's called double dipping! You either pick the pack or you get 4 cards if you are the first team to meld or 3 cards if you are the second team to meld.

responses.author: Patricia Imperial
responses.text: No

responses.author: Victoria Macy
responses.text: No

responses.author: Cathy Mennitt-Cadmus
responses.text: No

responses.author: Rita Hirshman Trokenheim
responses.text: No

recommendation: You CANNOT get both the talon and the pack — it's one or the other, never both. If you pick up the discard pile when melding, you forfeit your talon cards. If you meld without picking up the pile, you get your talon (4 cards for the first team to meld, 3 for the second).
strategy: You CANNOT get both the talon and the pack — it's one or the other, never both. If you pick up the discard pile when melding, you forfeit your talon cards. If you meld without picking up the pile, you get your talon (4 cards for the first team to meld, 3 for the second).

author: Michele Miele
question: Two questions: 1) Can a player going for a Straight special hand discard the extra Joker? 2) After picking up the pack, if left with only a 7 and dead cards, does she take back a melded card to discard?
context: Q1: Player building a Straight has only a Joker as extra card — can she discard it to avoid losing a needed card? Q2: After distributing pack cards to melds, player is left with a 7 and 2 dead cards (rank just closed) — none can go on an empty tray.

responses.author: Donna Wilson Nuss
responses.text: No, she can't discard a joker in this case. Yes, she has to take back a card she laid down. You could never put a seven or a dead card on an empty tray.

responses.author: Stephanie Adler Calliott
responses.text: 1) Unfortunately, she cannot discard that joker so her straight special hand is a no go. 2) She needs to pick up one of the cards she placed on her meld as she cannot discard the 7 or the two cards which are part of a closed meld.

responses.author: Christine Allan
responses.text: 1. We wouldn't allow the discard of the joker. 2. She would have to take back the last card she laid that was not a dead card to discard.

responses.author: Alysia Ehrlich Carrillo
responses.text: You cannot discard a joker. If this is the case start going for your points or another special. Yes she does have to take back a card, the last card you put down, to throw.

responses.author: Cheryl Dunne
responses.text: The joker cannot be discarded. Her attempt at the special hand is now ruined and she needs to pivot. After the pack is picked and there are no safe cards left to discard, the last safe card melded needs to be retrieved and discarded even if that card closes a Canasta.

responses.author: Caroline Dann
responses.text: No

recommendation: Q1: You can NEVER discard a Joker (or any wild card). If you're building a Straight special hand and your only extra card is a Joker, your special hand attempt is over — pivot to regular play or a different special. Q2: If after picking up the pack you have no legal discard (only 7s, 3s, or dead cards), you MUST take back the last safe card you melded and discard it instead, even if removing that card breaks a canasta.
strategy: Q1: You can NEVER discard a Joker (or any wild card). If you're building a Straight special hand and your only extra card is a Joker, your special hand attempt is over — pivot to regular play or a different special. Q2: If after picking up the pack you have no legal discard (only 7s, 3s, or dead cards), you MUST take back the last safe card you melded and discard it instead, even if removing that card breaks a canasta.

author: Wendy Klang
question: After laying down her original meld, a player only has a wild card left to discard. There's no natural card in any meld she can exchange it for. Can she discard the wild card, or does she take back all melds and get penalized?
context: Player opens her meld but her only remaining card is a wild card. No exchange possible with existing melds. What happens?

responses.author: Stephanie Adler Calliott
responses.text: Great question. Unfortunately, she has to pick everything up. And then it's an additional 10 pts to open. She can't discard the wild card.

responses.author: Kathy Weisman Wolf
responses.text: You can never throw a wild card

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: No. If that is all that is left then she has to pick up her melds. Cannot discard a wild.

responses.author: Rae Glaser
responses.text: No. She can't meld

responses.author: Caroline Dann
responses.text: No

recommendation: You can NEVER discard a wild card. If after laying down your opening meld your only remaining card is a wild, you must take back ALL your melds. The meld attempt fails, and you face an additional 10-point penalty added to your meld requirement for the next attempt. Always count your cards before melding to ensure you have a legal discard.
strategy: You can NEVER discard a wild card. If after laying down your opening meld your only remaining card is a wild, you must take back ALL your melds. The meld attempt fails, and you face an additional 10-point penalty added to your meld requirement for the next attempt. Always count your cards before melding to ensure you have a legal discard.

author: Merri Geri
question: I picked the last card and threw out a jack. Could the next player pick the pack?
context: Last card drawn from stock, player discards a jack instead of going out

responses.author: Stephanie Adler Calliott
responses.text: Great question. Yes. The next player has one last turn if they can pick the pack. Otherwise, the game ends. So that discard is still important.

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: Yes. Unless you have 2 completed canastas which means you can go out. If you do not have 2 completed canastas then if you throw one the next person can pick it up.

responses.author: Cheryl Troy
responses.text: Michelle, the last card (discarded) is placed face down only if that player goes out.

responses.author: Rae Glaser
responses.text: Yes. And when she throws out a card. Next person can pick. We had this happen

responses.author: Caroline Dann
responses.text: If you didn't go out- yes!

responses.author: Marleen Leventhal
responses.text: Absolutely YES

responses.author: Francine Feit
responses.text: Yes. If you didn't go out

responses.author: Gail Robinson Ragusa
responses.text: Yes!

responses.author: Donna Bottone Bianco
responses.text: Yes

responses.author: Gina Kaplan Katz
responses.text: Yes

responses.author: Neda Klein Couzens
responses.text: Yes

responses.author: Michelle Hodes Ehrman
responses.text: I thought when it's last card you could put it face down?

responses.author: Faye Scher
responses.text: [reply to Michelle] no. That's if you go out. Not picking the last card and discarding.

responses.author: Debby Glass Norwood
responses.text: Yes

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: Yup

responses.author: Bena Hoberman Weil
responses.text: Yes

responses.author: Rita Hirshman Trokenheim
responses.text: Yes

responses.author: Penny Cozza
responses.text: Yes

recommendation: Yes, the next player can pick the pack after the last stock card is drawn and discarded. The discard from the last stock card is treated like any normal discard - face up, and the next player gets one final turn to pick the pack if they can use the top card. The discard is only placed face down if the player goes out. Common confusion: some players conflate 'drawing the last card' with 'going out' but they are separate events.
strategy: Yes, the next player can pick the pack after the last stock card is drawn and discarded. The discard from the last stock card is treated like any normal discard - face up, and the next player gets one final turn to pick the pack if they can use the top card. The discard is only placed face down if the player goes out. Common confusion: some players conflate 'drawing the last card' with 'going out' but they are separate events.

author: Amy Beer Brown
question: If I am late with my initial meld and we are at the turn card, am I still allowed to take my three cards?
context: Player has not yet made initial meld, stock is at the turn card, asking about talon entitlement

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: If you are at the turn card you will get ONLY that turn card, no more.

responses.author: Joyce Altshuler Fox
responses.text: You can take the talon up to and including the turn card, that's it.

responses.author: Robin Levine
responses.text: You can take the turn card only but not the other 2 or 3 cards you'd ordinarily get for a Talon (depending upon whether you're the second or first melder)...

responses.author: Amy Elberger Meisler
responses.text: In your scenario you would only get the turn card.

responses.author: Bonnie Esserman Gershenzon
responses.text: Yes you can replace a 3 but you can't Toulon after the turn card. Confusing I know.

responses.author: Dianne Gianfelice
responses.text: Not after the turn card.

responses.author: Alix Kane
responses.text: You can take your talon up to and including the turn card. Nothing after that.

responses.author: Donna Bottone Bianco
responses.text: no. one card only

responses.author: Ruthie Trenk
responses.text: No, only the one for your turn.

responses.author: Rochelle Kirsch Friedland
responses.text: Only the turn card

recommendation: No, you cannot take a full talon (3 cards) when the stock has reached the turn card. If you are late making your initial meld and the stock is at the turn card, you only get the turn card as your draw - not the additional talon cards. The talon can only include cards up to and including the turn card; you cannot draw past it.
strategy: No, you cannot take a full talon (3 cards) when the stock has reached the turn card. If you are late making your initial meld and the stock is at the turn card, you only get the turn card as your draw - not the additional talon cards. The talon can only include cards up to and including the turn card; you cannot draw past it.

author: Andrew Freedman
question: If you already melded and draw a 3 in your talon, can you replace the 3 before drawing your next card — and if the replacement gives you a pair, can you pick the pack?
context: Player has melded and received a red 3 in their talon. On their next turn, they replace the 3 from the stock before drawing their regular card. If the replacement card gives them a matching pair to the top of the discard pile, can they pick the pack?

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: I tell this to people all the time. First replace the 3, then evaluate to pick a card or the pack. That first replacement card can make a big difference in next steps!

responses.author: Christine Allan
responses.text: We play you lay the 3 first because it was already in your hand. If that new card allows you to pick the pack, go for it.

responses.author: Caroline Dann
responses.text: I do a video on just that subject on insta gram @LAcanastaaclub. You must pause and say 'replacing for my 3' and then look and see if you now have a pair. Too many people lump the replacing for the 3 and their turn in one. You're possibly losing out on getting the pack.

responses.author: Dara Collins
responses.text: Yes! We encourage this as a Best Practice and Strategy since this may give you access to Pick the Pack.

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: We play that way. Told my groups that. But, thank you for taking the time to post this so others know the strategy.

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: Absolutely

responses.author: Kathy Weisman Wolf
responses.text: All the time!

responses.author: Andrew Freedman
responses.text: My buddies doubt me. I have to check the rule book to confirm, but glad other people are aware of it.

responses.author: Joanne Blau Bellet
responses.text: Here we go! Official rule or table rule?

responses.author: Jamie Lewin Meadow
responses.text: If you can't open and it's very close to the end of the hand, you have 3 red 3s on the table and you pick the 4th red 3, leave it in your hand. Take the penalty of a three in your hand instead of the extra 500 points for putting it on the table.

recommendation: When you draw a red 3 in your talon (after melding), you MUST replace it BEFORE your regular draw on your next turn. This is not just a rule — it's a key strategy. The replacement card might give you a pair matching the top of the discard pile, enabling you to pick the pack. Best practice: pause, announce 'replacing for my 3,' draw the replacement, THEN decide whether to draw from stock or pick the pack. Many players rush and lump the 3 replacement with their regular draw, missing the strategic opportunity.
bonus_strategy: Jamie Lewin Meadow shares a related tip: if you have 3 red 3s on the table and draw the 4th near the end of a hand when you haven't melded, consider keeping it in your hand. The -100 penalty for a 3 in hand is better than the -500 penalty for placing all 4 red 3s without having melded.
strategy: When you draw a red 3 in your talon (after melding), you MUST replace it BEFORE your regular draw on your next turn. This is not just a rule — it's a key strategy. The replacement card might give you a pair matching the top of the discard pile, enabling you to pick the pack. Best practice: pause, announce 'replacing for my 3,' draw the replacement, THEN decide whether to draw from stock or pick the pack. Many players rush and lump the 3 replacement with their regular draw, missing the strategic opportunity.

author: Adele Schubert Reinstein
question: If the last card in the deck is a three and you pick it, do you still throw out a card or does the game end?
context: Player draws the final card from the stock pile and it turns out to be a red 3. What happens next?

responses.author: Paula Rozan
responses.text: I've seen different approaches. Our table rule let the player get a discard. Unlike mahjong, canasta doesn't seem to have a 'final answer' that's carved in stone.

responses.author: Leslie Carol Berg Weiss
responses.text: In MAC if you pick the last card in the deck and it's a 3, the game ends right there and you don't get any further moves. You cannot add to any melds and you cannot go out.

responses.author: Rae Glaser
responses.text: Game ends. Do nothing.

recommendation: If you draw the last card from the stock and it's a red 3, the round ends immediately. You do not get a replacement card, you cannot add to melds, you cannot go out, and you do not discard. The 3 stays in your hand (penalized as an unplayed red 3). Some tables play differently as a house rule, but the MAC standard is clear: game over, no further action.
complexity_note: One respondent mentioned table rules differ, but the MAC-specific answer from Leslie Carol Berg Weiss is definitive.
strategy: If you draw the last card from the stock and it's a red 3, the round ends immediately. You do not get a replacement card, you cannot add to melds, you cannot go out, and you do not discard. The 3 stays in your hand (penalized as an unplayed red 3). Some tables play differently as a house rule, but the MAC standard is clear: game over, no further action.

author: Leslie Carol Berg Weiss
question: Partner picked the pack with an Ace on top to initially open. He opened with dirty Aces and another clean set. Friend says he cannot do that because Aces must be clean to initially open. Who is correct?
context: Player picks up the discard pile using the Ace on top to make their initial meld, but melds dirty Aces (Aces + wild card). Question is whether Aces must be clean when picking the pack to open.

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: When you are opening your meld with an ace, it has to be clean. When picking the pack. Aces cannot be a meld of aces with a wild card.. good luck

responses.author: Robin Hammer
responses.text: The points on the ACE cannot be counted in the opening.

responses.author: Donna Wilson Nuss
responses.text: Your friend is correct.

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: Cannot do that. Unless Ace's melded dirty already if picked from discard must be laid down clean.

responses.author: Richard Kennett
responses.text: You can open with dirty aces on initial meld, but CANNOT pick the pack to meld with dirty aces.

responses.author: Tanya Wolff Deutsch
responses.text: Nope Must be clean if you pick the pack with the Ace to open.

responses.author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
responses.text: I don't think you are allowed to pick up the pack if you meld with dirty aces and you have the right amount of your RPC... you can only pick up the pack on your next round providing you have 2 aces in your hand.

responses.author: Amy Pressler
responses.text: Must be clean.

responses.author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
responses.text: Aces must be clean if you are picking the pack to meld..

responses.author: Leslie Carol Berg Weiss
responses.text: Found the rule on page 37!! We cannot pick the pack to open with dirty aces. Thank you all!

recommendation: You can open with dirty aces on initial meld, but CANNOT pick the pack to meld with dirty aces.
strategy: You can open with dirty aces on initial meld, but CANNOT pick the pack to meld with dirty aces.

author: Lynda Garber
question: Is a Splash a special hand? Is it worth 500 or 3500 points? Does it end the round?
context: Player thought Splash was just a term for opening with a red canasta (7 clean cards) worth 500 points. Someone told her it's worth 3500 points and ends the round like a special hand.

responses.author: Beth F. Levine
responses.text: There are so many people who do not play by MAC rules. According to MAC rules, a splash is a clean / pure Canasta (7 cards, no wilds) that meets all the meld points requirements for opening (125, 155, or 180). It is worth 500 points when counting base scores.

responses.author: Arlene Satenspiel
responses.text: You are right. 500 Does not end game.

responses.author: Bonnie Ashley
responses.text: Hard to get and only 500 points. It's satisfies any melding round.

responses.author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
responses.text: A clean canasta to open is called a splash is worth only 500 points - it doesn't end the game - it's special but not a special hand that would end the game - I never heard of it worth 3500 points.

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: 7 wilds = 2,000 points [shared image of scoring reference]

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: I did 2 years ago with 4's! Looking for that picture

recommendation: A Splash is opening your initial meld with a clean/pure canasta (7 natural cards of the same rank, no wild cards). It is worth 500 points (the standard clean canasta bonus) and satisfies ANY meld point requirement (125, 155, or 180). A Splash does NOT end the round — it is not a special hand like a Straight or Pairs. Play continues normally after a Splash.
strategy: A Splash is opening your initial meld with a clean/pure canasta (7 natural cards of the same rank, no wild cards). It is worth 500 points (the standard clean canasta bonus) and satisfies ANY meld point requirement (125, 155, or 180). A Splash does NOT end the round — it is not a special hand like a Straight or Pairs. Play continues normally after a Splash.

author: Merri Geri
question: We played to the last card and no one went out. As her last discard, can my partner discard a Joker so we don't have those 50 negative points in hand?
context: Stock is depleted, round is ending. Player wants to strategically discard a Joker on their final turn to avoid the -50 point penalty for having it in hand.

responses.author: Lynne De Los Santos
responses.text: No, unless she has nothing except wildcards in her hand.

responses.author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
responses.text: No, she had other cards in her hand to discard - You can only discard if you have no other choice and you only have jokers or wild.

responses.author: Penny Goldner Reich
responses.text: You are not really 'going out'. You are just playing until the last card. That is not going out. So no, you cannot throw a wild card.

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: Only if she is holding only wilds in her hand. And, the opponent still has the opportunity to pick the pack with your teams last card discarded.

responses.author: Sandra Lerner
responses.text: No unless all she has in her hand are wildcards.

recommendation: You CANNOT strategically discard a Joker (or any wild card) just to avoid holding it for negative points. Wild cards can only be discarded when you have NO other choice — meaning your entire hand consists of only wild cards with no natural cards left to discard. The 'no discarding wilds' rule applies at ALL times, including end-of-round play when the stock is depleted. The only exception is when wilds are literally all you have left.
strategy: You CANNOT strategically discard a Joker (or any wild card) just to avoid holding it for negative points. Wild cards can only be discarded when you have NO other choice — meaning your entire hand consists of only wild cards with no natural cards left to discard. The 'no discarding wilds' rule applies at ALL times, including end-of-round play when the stock is depleted. The only exception is when wilds are literally all you have left.

author: Joe Santoro Jr.
question: When you're playing 1 vs 1, how do you put down special hands? You're picking up 2 cards on every draw.
context: Player asking about whether special hands exist in 2-player (1v1) Canasta, since the dealing and drawing mechanics differ from 4-player.

responses.author: Judy Jacoby Riback
responses.text: No special hands when playing 1 vs 1.

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: No special hands in 2 player canasta. 15 cards each & pick 2 cards each!

responses.author: Joe Santoro Jr.
responses.text: ok that's what I figured. Thanks

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: anytime

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: we play to 10k points in 1v1

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: that's your table rule.. good going!

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: There are no special hands!

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: No special hands

recommendation: There are NO special hands in 2-player (1v1) Canasta. The 2-player variant uses different rules: each player is dealt 15 cards (instead of 11) and draws 2 cards per turn (instead of 1). Special hands (Straights, Pairs, Garbage, etc.) are only available in the standard 4-player game. Some tables play to 10,000 points in 1v1, but that's a table rule rather than standard.
strategy: There are NO special hands in 2-player (1v1) Canasta. The 2-player variant uses different rules: each player is dealt 15 cards (instead of 11) and draws 2 cards per turn (instead of 1). Special hands (Straights, Pairs, Garbage, etc.) are only available in the standard 4-player game. Some tables play to 10,000 points in 1v1, but that's a table rule rather than standard.

author: Anita Pfeil
question: I had three queens, two jacks and a joker, and 2 10's and a deuce (130 points). A queen was discarded. I was told I couldn't pick the pile because I needed two EXTRA queens. What is the correct ruling?
context: Player had not yet melded. Had 3 queens, jacks+joker, 10s+deuce totaling 130 points. Top discard was a queen. Was told she needed 2 extra queens (4 total) in hand to pick the pack. This appears to be a Florida-specific table rule vs MAC rules.

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: Florida makes up rules unto themselves and they think that their rules are rules of God, which isn't true! With that being said, if you have the correct meld of 125, 155, 180, in points in your hand to meld, absolutely yes you can pick up the pack and add it to your meld after you have melded. Good luck

responses.author: Sherry Davis Wainz
responses.text: Such is the challenge with canasta. I learned based on a Naples FL group's rules but I am aware that that is not how MAC teaches. I have to remember where I am when I am playing and confirm the rules. Having said that either group could pick the pack if required meld was met without the top card.

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: So they are saying you needed FOUR Queens to take the pack? Balderdash!

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: This is a good question. I've played in groups that play both ways. Probably best to ask this question BEFORE initial meld if you are playing with a new group.

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: We play that you meld with the points you need. You have a clean 3 and other cards to make your meld points. Once you meld with say 125 points you can pick the pack. You don't get your 3 or 4 card talon.

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: According to how I was taught - you were correct

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: Based on what you are telling me and the rules my girls play by you are correct. You can take the pack.

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: You had 140 points including your clean triple in your hand. You can 100% pick up the pack.

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: No [extra queens needed]. As long as you had the necessary points without counting the card on top of the pack.

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: Not true. Unless the table rule is need an alien pair. And that usually can be any pair. Yes you can pick the deck.

recommendation: You do NOT need extra queens beyond a natural pair to pick the pack for your initial meld. The requirements are: (1) Have a natural pair matching the top discard card (2 queens matching the discarded queen — she had 3 queens, so she easily qualifies), and (2) Meet the meld point requirement (125/155/180) from cards in your hand WITHOUT counting the top discard card.  Always confirm house rules when playing with a new group, especially in Florida where regional variations are common.
complexity_note: Some Florida groups require an 'alien pair' (any natural pair separate from the matching pair) to pick the pack. This is a table rule, not MAC standard.
strategy: You do NOT need extra queens beyond a natural pair to pick the pack for your initial meld. The requirements are: (1) Have a natural pair matching the top discard card (2 queens matching the discarded queen — she had 3 queens, so she easily qualifies), and (2) Meet the meld point requirement (125/155/180) from cards in your hand WITHOUT counting the top discard card.  Always confirm house rules when playing with a new group, especially in Florida where regional variations are common.

author: Sheryl Robin
question: A player says a team does not join their 3's until one person on the team has melded, reducing the penalty if neither melds. Is this a real rule?
context: Player was told that black 3s are kept separate per player until someone melds, which would reduce the team penalty if neither player melds (e.g., 2 individual 3s = 100+100 vs 2 combined 3s = 300). She can't find this in the MAC rule book.

responses.author: Faye Scher
responses.text: Not true. Not even a house rule. It's a team game. As far as both having three aces, you are both penalized. The team part is the board.

responses.author: Beth F. Levine
responses.text: Nope. Not true in MAC. In MAC, the first team member to get a 3 places it on the table. If/when the second team member gets a 3 it goes over with the first one. Once the first meld is laid on the table by that team, all 3s move to that player and all future pulled 3s go with them. If no canastas are made before the round ends, the penalties are incurred for all 3s for that team.

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: That is not correct. These are separate point counts. If your opponent goes out, you put your 3s together to count the total, positive or negative. In hand penalties for 7s or As are counted based on what each player has in their hand.

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: You are a team therefore everything is counted together.. 3's, more than 2 aces, more than 2 sevens.

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: Threes count as a team but your hand minuses are individually counted.

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: It's not in the book because it's not a ruling. It's standard practice. If you do not open you each keep your threes until someone opens and melds. Then you combine your threes: 1-3 = 100pts, 2-threes = 300pts, 3-threes = 500, 4-threes = 1000 points.

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: It's a team game so whatever 3's you have and your partner has it is combined together in scoring. Comparing aces? If your partner holds 2 Aces and you have 2 aces in your hand you don't combine it other than point value of 20 points negative.

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: The only thing you combine are your 3's

responses.author: Unknown
responses.text: That is false. You play as a team so what is yours is your partner's.

analysis.sub_debate_hands: split on whether hand penalties (Aces/7s) are combined or individual
