recommendation: Black 3s are ALWAYS combined as a team for scoring. The claim that teams keep 3s separate until someone melds (to reduce penalties) is NOT a rule. The physical practice of each player placing their own 3s on the table is for tracking purposes only; at scoring time, ALL team 3s are counted together using the escalating penalty scale (1=100, 2=300, 3=500, 4=1000). Note: There IS a secondary debate about whether in-hand penalty cards (Aces, 7s held at round end) are counted individually per player or combined as a team — the community leans toward individual counting for hands but combined for 3s laid down.
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: While there's a sub-debate on hand penalties for Aces/7s, the core question about 3s has unanimous consensus — they're always combined as a team.
strategy: Black 3s are ALWAYS combined as a team for scoring. The claim that teams keep 3s separate until someone melds (to reduce penalties) is NOT a rule. The physical practice of each player placing their own 3s on the table is for tracking purposes only; at scoring time, ALL team 3s are counted together using the escalating penalty scale (1=100, 2=300, 3=500, 4=1000). Note: There IS a secondary debate about whether in-hand penalty cards (Aces, 7s held at round end) are counted individually per player or combined as a team — the community leans toward individual counting for hands but combined for 3s laid down.

author: Alberta Catherine
question: If you have a wild card canasta at the end do you count the points inside the canasta? What do they just count with the total points for the actual canasta?
context: Player unsure whether the individual card point values inside a wild card canasta are counted in addition to the canasta bonus, or if only the canasta bonus applies.

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: Having a wild card Canasta it's just like having any other Canasta. 2,000 points for mixed wilds, 2,500 points for 4 big wilds, 3,000 points for all 2's. Now that you've counted your canastas like any other Canasta count what's inside each Canasta is and please don't forget after every hundred count you put a three on top.

responses.author: Faye Scher
responses.text: Yes, you get the points in the card count, just like 7's or Aces

responses.author: Beth F. Levine
responses.text: Yes. Remember, there are two scores for each round (added together). The base score is your 3s plus Canastas (including wilds, Aces &/ or 7s). The second score is your points for all cards left on the table (including point values for individual cards in any Canasta)

responses.author: Rae Glaser
responses.text: Yes

responses.author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
responses.text: Yes, you get your 2000 or 2500 points- after you count your wilds with your other cards

responses.author: Gayle Kollitz
responses.text: You always count the canastas and you always count the cards. It doesn't matter how many points the canasta is worth, the card count is separate from the base count.

recommendation: YES, you count BOTH the canasta bonus AND the individual card points inside a wild card canasta. Scoring has two separate components added together each round: (1) BASE SCORE: 3s bonuses + canasta bonuses (wild card canasta: 2,000 for mixed wilds, 2,500 for 4 big jokers, 3,000 for all 2's), and (2) CARD COUNT: point values of every individual card on the table, including all cards inside canastas. Wild card canastas are scored exactly like any other canasta — you get the bonus plus the card values inside.
strategy: YES, you count BOTH the canasta bonus AND the individual card points inside a wild card canasta. Scoring has two separate components added together each round: (1) BASE SCORE: 3s bonuses + canasta bonuses (wild card canasta: 2,000 for mixed wilds, 2,500 for 4 big jokers, 3,000 for all 2's), and (2) CARD COUNT: point values of every individual card on the table, including all cards inside canastas. Wild card canastas are scored exactly like any other canasta — you get the bonus plus the card values inside.

author: Robin Shane Rashkind
question: When opening up without picking the pile, needed 125. Placed 125 on the table and only one card left was told I needed one to hold one to throw. I thought I could just throw my one left and pick 4 but I was told no. Who is correct?
context: Player melded 125 points from hand without picking the pack, leaving only one card. Was told she needed to keep a card in hand (one to discard + one to hold). She thought she could discard her last card and then draw a talon of 3-4 cards. 47 comments with significant debate.

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: It is fine to have one card left to discard and nothing remaining in your hand as long as you are able to pick a card or cards for your talon. You do not need to have one card to discard and one card remaining in your hand.

responses.author: Andi Stone Katz
responses.text: You can discard the last card and pick or 3 or 4 talon.

responses.author: Kathy Weisman Wolf
responses.text: In my game(s) you can throw out your last card IF it's before the turn card and you get a talon. If it is after the turn card then you must hold a card in your hand because you don't get a talon.

responses.author: Beth F. Levine
responses.text: If it's well before the turn card, one left to throw is fine since you will pick up 3 or 4 cards in the talon. If after the turn card, you don't pick up a talon so you need 2 cards left after initial meld; one to discard & one to keep in your hand.

responses.author: Jamie Lewin Meadow
responses.text: TABLE RULE (too many table rules)

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: You need one to discard as long as you can pick up a card. U cannot discard if you can't pick up a card.

responses.author: Barbara Magliozzi
responses.text: As beyond turn card, you get no talon so they are correct.

responses.author: Rita Hirshman Trokenheim
responses.text: Table rule

responses.author: Patti Heleva
responses.text: If you've melded (but before picking your talon), we play that you must have two cards left in your hand.... one to throw and one to hold. If picking the pile when melding, you do not have to put a card on the table face down to discard... you can discard any card from your hand.

responses.author: Robin Shane Rashkind
responses.text: So one says yes and one says no! Is there a definitive answer or is it more of a table rule

recommendation: The answer depends on the TURN CARD. BEFORE the turn card: You CAN discard your last card after melding, because you will draw a talon (3-4 cards) which gives you cards to continue. You do NOT need to keep one card in hand plus one to discard — discarding your last card is fine as long as a talon is available. AFTER the turn card: You do NOT get a talon, so you MUST keep at least one card in hand after discarding. This means you need 2 cards remaining after your initial meld (one to discard + one to keep).
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: While some call this entirely a table rule, the dominant community consensus draws a clear line at the turn card. Before turn card = can discard last card (talon available). After turn card = must keep a card (no talon).
strategy: The answer depends on the TURN CARD. BEFORE the turn card: You CAN discard your last card after melding, because you will draw a talon (3-4 cards) which gives you cards to continue. You do NOT need to keep one card in hand plus one to discard — discarding your last card is fine as long as a talon is available. AFTER the turn card: You do NOT get a talon, so you MUST keep at least one card in hand after discarding. This means you need 2 cards remaining after your initial meld (one to discard + one to keep).

author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
question: My partner was ready to go out. She asked if she can. Her card that she was discarding was a dead card. I said yes she was able too. It was a safe card plus when she discards you turn the card over and no one had to see it.
context: Top contributor sharing that when going out, the final discard is placed face down and any card can be used. Not really a question — more of a statement/tip that prompted discussion. 14 comments.

responses.author: Kim Taber
responses.text: Is there a question in here?

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: Absolutely. Any card can be the face down card to go out.

responses.author: Sandra Lerner
responses.text: Yes

responses.author: Donna Bottone Bianco
responses.text: Correct

responses.author: Kathy Weisman Wolf
responses.text: 100% correct!

responses.author: Barbara Fisher Levine
responses.text: Unless the discard pile was empty, why do you care if last card is a dead card? Isn't it safe to throw it out? Regardless of it being right side up or facing down. Am I wrong?

responses.author: Louise Horowitz Shapiro
responses.text: When do you need a card in your hand and one to throw

responses.author: Donna Cardella
responses.text: Thank you all for helping me learn the game.

recommendation: When going out, your final discard is placed FACE DOWN. This means ANY card can be used as the final discard — dead card, live card, it doesn't matter since opponents never see it. The key rule: going out = final discard face down, any card works.
strategy: When going out, your final discard is placed FACE DOWN. This means ANY card can be used as the final discard — dead card, live card, it doesn't matter since opponents never see it. The key rule: going out = final discard face down, any card works.

author: Ellen Leikind
question: What is your strategy for how soon you open when at 125 level? Online virtual partner opens on 1st or 2nd round? Seems too soon to me. What are your guidelines?
context: Top contributor asking about optimal timing for initial meld at the 125-point threshold. Notes that online/virtual partners open very early (1st or 2nd round) which seems premature. 6 comments with strategy advice.

responses.author: Lori Drucker
responses.text: Many factors. Lots of 3's. Or have good hand to go down and out. Also it's computer generated.

responses.author: Ellen Leikind
responses.text: Thanks Lori. All good reasons but what about if those situations don't apply. I tend to hold off and try to pick the pack. Is that overly risky or a reasonable strategy?

responses.author: Patti Heleva
responses.text: If I have enough points to meld, it depends upon how far into the deck we are. If it's early in the deck, I'll wait 3-4 passes...maaybe 5 passes and then I'll meld. If we're half way or more through the deck I'll wait 1-2 passes and then meld. If I see the other team counting cards to meld (but can't), I'll meld immediately to get the extra card in the talon. So different scenarios produce different actions.

responses.author: Chris Bradley
responses.text: Depends on where the game is. If opponents have opened, I would go down as soon as I could. But if the opponents haven't opened and you have several pairs, I would hold off for perhaps two hands in hopes of picking the pack while opening. Take my advice with a grain of salt. I am a newer player and still figuring things out.

responses.author: Terri Golden
responses.text: If I have lots of pairs, I will hold off a little assuming the other team has not opened.

responses.author: Caroline Dann
responses.text: It depends on so many things. There is not one answer.

recommendation: Opening strategy at the 125-meld level is SITUATIONAL, not one-size-fits-all. Key factors to consider: (1) DECK POSITION: Early in the deck (before halfway) — wait 3-5 passes before melding to build a stronger hand and pursue the pack. Late in the deck — meld within 1-2 passes, don't risk running out of cards. (2) OPPONENT STATUS: If opponents have already melded — open as soon as you can to avoid falling further behind. If opponents haven't melded — holding off is more viable. (3) PAIRS IN HAND: More pairs = more incentive to hold off and try to pick the pack. (4) OPPONENT BODY LANGUAGE: If you see opponents counting cards to meld, meld immediately to claim the talon advantage. Opening on the 1st or 2nd round (as virtual partners do) is generally too early, but this is more a strategy question than a rule question.
strategy: Opening strategy at the 125-meld level is SITUATIONAL, not one-size-fits-all. Key factors to consider: (1) DECK POSITION: Early in the deck (before halfway) — wait 3-5 passes before melding to build a stronger hand and pursue the pack. (4) OPPONENT BODY LANGUAGE: If you see opponents counting cards to meld, meld immediately to claim the talon advantage. author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
question: If my partner melds with dirty Aces (she had 4) and a triple but did not have enough points and picked them up, can I then meld with dirty Aces? Also, if I later pick the deck and get several Aces, can I try to make clean Aces knowing what my partner had?
context: Partner attempted a mismeld with dirty aces but lacked sufficient points and had to retract. Two questions: (1) Can the other partner now meld dirty aces? (2) Can anyone on the team pursue clean aces after the mismeld revealed partner's aces? 6 comments with good debate.

responses.author: Terri Golden
responses.text: Yes, you can generally meld dirty (mixed) aces if your partner previously mismelded aces, provided your team can meet the required, often increased, point count for the initial meld. While you cannot typically lay down pure aces after your partner has exposed them in an incorrect, premature, or 'dirty' manner, a new, valid dirty ace meld is allowed, often with a 10-point penalty applied to your score.

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: Another question: what if I picked up the deck with a 5 and inside that pile were 5 Aces and I had one, could I put that down to try to make a clean Ace canasta?

responses.author: Donna Wilson Nuss
responses.text: No, you can't meld clean aces because you know your partner has aces because of the mismeld.

responses.author: Paula Rozan
responses.text: That's why our table rule says: if you mismeld, then you must be the one to meld with the extra 10 points.

responses.author: Jamie Lewin Meadow
responses.text: No clean aces after the mismeld. Same with wilds and 7's. Ex: If your partner mismelds with 3 wilds you cannot put your wilds down for a wild canasta.

responses.author: Jill Klaskin Press
responses.text: No and no

recommendation: After a partner's MISMELD that reveals aces: CLEAN ACES — Absolutely NOT. Once a mismeld exposes what your partner holds, the 'knowledge contamination' principle kicks in: you CANNOT meld clean versions of those cards because you now know your partner has them. This extends to ALL restricted melds — aces, wilds, and 7s. If a partner mismelds with wilds, no one on the team can pursue a wild canasta. DIRTY ACES — More debated. The safer play is to assume dirty aces CAN be melded by the non-mismelding partner, but with the standard mismeld penalty applied. Some groups add table rules (e.g., the mismelder must be the one to re-meld with the extra points).
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: The clean aces answer is clear (NO). For dirty aces after a mismeld, the most common interpretation is YES with penalty, though some groups say no. When in doubt, the mismelder should attempt the re-meld themselves.
strategy: After a partner's MISMELD that reveals aces: CLEAN ACES — Absolutely NOT. Once a mismeld exposes what your partner holds, the 'knowledge contamination' principle kicks in: you CANNOT meld clean versions of those cards because you now know your partner has them. This extends to ALL restricted melds — aces, wilds, and 7s. If a partner mismelds with wilds, no one on the team can pursue a wild canasta. DIRTY ACES — More debated. The safer play is to assume dirty aces CAN be melded by the non-mismelding partner, but with the standard mismeld penalty applied. Some groups add table rules (e.g., the mismelder must be the one to re-meld with the extra points).

author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
question: I was just told that in zip code wilds aren't allowed.. is that a table rule?
context: Top contributor was told wild cards cannot be used in zip code special hands. Asking if this is an official rule or a table rule. 20 comments with overwhelming response.

responses.author: Donna Wilson Nuss
responses.text: They are allowed as long as they match.

responses.author: Mina Dalimonte
responses.text: Must be a table rule…I was told unless they match you cannot use as a wild.

responses.author: Gayle Kollitz
responses.text: Most people allow matching wilds in zip code hand.

responses.author: Merri Geri
responses.text: There are many different 'canasta' special hands handouts. Many are slightly different. If we are playing with someone new we say these are the special hands we follow. If the hand says no wild cards or aces, then that is it.

responses.author: Barbara Schnur
responses.text: Table rule

responses.author: Marcia Weisman
responses.text: Table rule. Before you sit down at a table, you might want to ask them if they have any table rules. That could alleviate confusion.

responses.author: Dara Collins
responses.text: Most widely played Zip Code permits Wild Cards however since the hand is so commonly made, many groups prevent using Wild Cards.

responses.author: April Volponi
responses.text: Never heard this -- and not in anything written that I've seen

responses.author: Lara Rush
responses.text: Yes table rule. For years when I lived in one city we only played you could not have any wilds in a zip code but now in atlanta they play you can use wilds in a zip code so that's how I play.

responses.author: Paula Rozan
responses.text: Table rule. We allow.

responses.author: Lynne De Los Santos
responses.text: Table rule to make getting zip code harder.

responses.author: Lynda Greenberg
responses.text: We play NO WILDS ..too easy with wild cards.

responses.author: Caryn Hirsch Coates
responses.text: We don't play that anymore, too easy.

responses.author: Debbie Solomon
responses.text: Yes!

responses.author: Gail Nusbaum Schwartz
responses.text: I play you can.

responses.author: Donna Bottone Bianco
responses.text: We allow it.

responses.author: Joanne Blau Bellet
responses.text: Table rule. Some groups don't even play zip code bc they think it's too easy!

responses.author: Harriet Wilner
responses.text: Another table rule.

responses.author: Tammy Max
responses.text: Table rule.

responses.author: Judy Winter
responses.text: Yes.

analysis.key_insight: Banning wilds in zip code is a common TABLE RULE adopted by groups who find the hand too easy. The default play allows matching wilds.

recommendation: Wild cards ARE allowed in zip code special hands by default — as long as they MATCH the denomination of the set they're placed in. This is the most widely played version. However, banning wilds from zip code is one of the most COMMON TABLE RULES in Canasta. Groups adopt this rule because zip code is considered one of the easier special hands, and removing wilds makes it significantly harder to achieve. Some groups go even further and remove zip code entirely from their special hands list. BEFORE sitting down at any table: always ask about table rules, especially regarding special hands. The key takeaway — 'no wilds in zip code' is NOT an official rule, it's a widespread table modification.

author: Gloryann Torres
question: I had a special hand of 3-3-3-3-2. one player said the pair had to be wild cards. is she correct?
context: Player had a special hand consisting of four sets of 3 and one pair of 2. Another player claimed the pair in such a special hand must be wild cards. 44 comments with strong debate between MAC rules (wilds required) and table rules (any pair).

responses.author: Faye Scher
responses.text: Deuces or wilds (matching pair)

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: yes this is correct. the pair must be wilds - 2s or Js

responses.author: Paula Rozan
responses.text: Table rule. Up to your game. I always ask before I sub in a different game. On canasta junction- must be wild cards. On my game, they allow any pair

responses.author: Beverly Chassen Resnick
responses.text: These various table rules are driving me crazy.

responses.author: Marcia Weisman
responses.text: It depends whether you play by your own table rules or whether you follow Modern American Canasta.

responses.author: Diana Hibbard LoCascio
responses.text: You can also have a set of 3 of the other kind of wilds but the pair must be 2 wilds.

responses.author: Leslie Carol Berg Weiss
responses.text: Yes. But that hand is not an MAC hand

responses.author: Susie Linder Rozanczyk
responses.text: In my Game no. I have never heard of that rule

responses.author: Toby Gopen
responses.text: Wow really mixed comments, I will just say congrats on a great hand!!!!

responses.author: Judy Hoffman Connick
responses.text: We had a pairs hand … can it be 77 AA 22 22 ?

analysis.key_insight: MAC official rules require wilds for the pair in special hands, but many home games allow any pair

recommendation: In Modern American Canasta (MAC) official rules, the pair in a special hand like 3-3-3-3-2 MUST be wild cards (2s or Jokers). This is the standard MAC requirement. However, many home games and table rules allow ANY matching pair in special hands — this is one of the most common table rule variations. Canasta Junction (online) also enforces wilds-only for the pair. NOTE: Some players pointed out that 3-3-3-3-2 is not an official MAC special hand, which adds another layer of table-rule variation. RECOMMENDATION: If playing MAC rules, the pair must be wilds. If playing at a new table, always ask about their special hand rules before playing.
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: Despite the split, the clear answer is that MAC rules require wild pairs in special hands. Table rules may differ, but always ask before sitting down.

author: Ronald Feingold
question: A player melded with four wild cards for over 125 points. Can the player or his partner add in a wildcard to another meld? The player in our game proceeded to add a wildcard to 2 10's. Is that permitted?
context: Player opened by melding 4 wild cards (meeting the 125 meld requirement). Then attempted to add a wild card to a separate meld of 2 10s. Question is whether wilds can go to other melds once a wild card canasta is started. 23 comments with 1 share. Note: this is in the general Canasta Card Game group, not MAC-specific.

responses.author: Robin Krant
responses.text: No. The wild can only go on the wild card canasta

responses.author: Michelle Krumper-Steinberg
responses.text: No. All wild must go on the wild canasta until it is closed.

responses.author: Mindy Morgan
responses.text: You have to have more natural cards than wilds but wild cards can be played on any canasta

responses.author: Wendy Lewis
responses.text: You can put 2 cards of the same number with a wild card - yes

analysis.key_insight: In MAC rules once a wild canasta is started all wilds must go there until closed. In classic/traditional Canasta wilds can go on any meld as long as naturals exceed wilds.

recommendation: This depends entirely on which version of Canasta you're playing. In MODERN AMERICAN CANASTA (MAC): Once a wild card canasta is started, ALL wild cards MUST be placed on that wild canasta until it is closed (7 cards). You CANNOT add wilds to other melds while a wild canasta is open. In CLASSIC/TRADITIONAL CANASTA: Wild cards can be added to any meld, as long as the meld maintains more natural cards than wild cards. Both versions agree on one thing: you always need more natural cards than wilds in a regular meld. RECOMMENDATION: Clarify which ruleset your group is playing before the game starts. If playing MAC, wilds are locked to the wild canasta once started.
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: The split is version-dependent, not a true disagreement. MAC = wilds locked, Classic = wilds flexible. Know your version.

author: Kathy Jokl
question: If you pick the pile and can then go out on the same turn, does your final discard have to meet the 'empty tray' requirements? (Not a dead card, not a 7 or ace or wild)
context: Rising contributor asking whether the 'empty tray' discard restrictions (no dead cards, no 7s, aces, or wilds) apply to the FINAL discard when going out, given that going out means the card is placed face down. 28 comments, overwhelmingly unanimous.

responses.author: Kim Schermer Fetterman
responses.text: Nope! Any card can be thrown as it is turned upside down.

responses.author: Lara Rush
responses.text: No. When you go out you put the card face down. Can be any card

responses.author: Shoshana Fluss Pilevsky
responses.text: Any card can be discarded as your last card- you turn your card over and no one sees it

responses.author: Donna Bottone Bianco
responses.text: No. Face down. No one sees it

responses.author: Diane Goldstein Horowitz
responses.text: Any card turned face down.

responses.author: Kim Raso Stewart
responses.text: I have gone out with a wild card because it is turned over. It's a bonafide rule, not a table rule.

responses.author: Cheryl Stein Herman
responses.text: Any card could be thrown out it's face down

responses.author: Lynne De Los Santos
responses.text: No. When you go out, the card is face down, so it can be any card. EXCEPT a 3. A 3 can never be thrown under any circumstance.

responses.author: Mickie Galanter Applebaum
responses.text: On an empty tray??? We have always played it still needs to be a legal discard for an empty tray. Is everyone here saying it doesn't????

responses.author: Amy Elberger Meisler
responses.text: No, when you go out you throw your card face down. You can throw any card you like.

analysis.key_insight: Overwhelming consensus that going out overrides empty tray restrictions because the card is face down. One minor exception noted: 3s can never be discarded even face down.

recommendation: When going out, your final discard is placed FACE DOWN and can be ANY card — wilds, aces, 7s, dead cards, anything. The 'empty tray' discard restrictions DO NOT apply to the final going-out discard. This is an official rule, not a table rule. The logic: since the card is face down and no one sees it, the discard restrictions are irrelevant. ONE EXCEPTION: Red and black 3s can never be discarded under any circumstance — this applies even when going out. Kim Raso Stewart specifically emphasized this is 'a bonafide rule, not a table rule.' Only 1 of 28 commenters disagreed, believing empty tray rules still apply when going out — this appears to be a house rule, not the standard.

author: Millie Arana
question: Q1: Is it important to watch what your partner discards and if so why? Q2: If your teammate went down and I have 4 or 5 wilds is it better to build on my teammates melds or start a wild card meld?
context: Top contributor asking two strategy questions: (1) should you pay attention to partner's discards, and (2) when your partner has already melded, should you add wilds to existing melds or start a wild card canasta?

responses.author: Faye Scher
responses.text: 1. You need to watch what everyone is throwing. You need to know so you don't get stuck with 7s and aces, or possibly pick the pack to get 7s etc for a canasta. Also, if your partner throws a 7, it could mean she had three of them. 2. If your partner went down and used wilds, there is a greater chance you won't close wilds. She probably has no more. I would build other started canastas as the game progresses.

responses.author: Chris Bradley
responses.text: Well, this is not coming from an expert so listen to what others say, but to me, what your partner throws is a hint of cards she doesn't have multiples of, so I would probably decide that card wouldn't be a building block for our team. For your second question, you need to see what wilds are exposed-how many are exposed-but I can say that I wouldn't go down with just 4. If my partner didn't need wilds to meld, I might consider doing it with 5. FWIW

responses.author: Heidi Sue
responses.text: You must be aware of what every person is throwing. Could it be a card to throw you off? By melding 3 holding back 2 to pick up the pack? It's taking a chance! How many 7s, As thrown. Try and remember to count. It depends on when to start to unload those wilds. If the other team needs 180 & hasn't melded yet, go for melding and go out ASAP! If both teams melded, wait and see how the game progresses. See what you can close in fifth or sixth position before just adding wilds to get points. How many canastas each team has. It's instinct.

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: you want to be aware of all cards discarded by both your partner and your opponent. This may help decide if you should pick the pack at any point - knowing where you may complete a canasta or pick a pack that is too dirty based on where you are in the hand. I would not play a wild card canasta after your partner has opened - it is likely they've used their wilds to open so closing it may be more difficult

responses.author: Millie Arana
responses.text: Thank you both.

responses.author: Lynda Balzer D'Alessio
responses.text: Good answer.

recommendation: Two key strategy principles: WATCHING DISCARDS: You MUST track what EVERYONE discards — both partners and opponents. Partner discards signal cards they don't hold multiples of (weak suits for your team). Opponent discards help you identify safe throws and canasta opportunities. Pay special attention to 7s and Aces being discarded — they reveal who's collecting restricted cards. Some discards may be deceptive (melding 3, holding 2 to pick the pack later). WILDS DECISION: If your partner already melded using wild cards, do NOT start a wild card canasta — your partner likely used their wilds to open, making it very hard to complete 7. Instead, use your wilds to build toward closing existing canastas (aim for 5th-6th position cards to complete). Only consider starting a wild canasta with 5+ wilds AND if your partner didn't use wilds to meld. The decision is also situational: if opponents need 180 and haven't melded, push to go out fast; if both teams are melded, play strategically for canasta closings.

author: Lori Mowery Weakland
question: If both teams melded with same cards and one team closes them we play that you can still add to it even though it won't close. Some play that once it's closed the other team cannot add to theirs. Thoughts?
context: Both teams have melded the same card rank. One team closes a canasta of that rank. Can the other team still add cards of that rank to their own incomplete meld? 44 comments.

responses.author: Robin Levine
responses.text: Once a Canasta is closed you cannot add to your own meld of that Canasta that you tabled before it closed... that's not a table rule and we play with no one who does that... where are all these individual table rules coming from?

responses.author: Dana Ross
responses.text: Once one team closed a canasta the opponent can no longer add to their open meld of the same card. That card is now dead, and can be a safe discard - but not into an empty discard tray if the pack has been picked!

responses.author: Alix Kane
responses.text: Dead is dead. Once the other team closes it, your team cannot add to the same number in your meld.

responses.author: Stacey Perlin Labell
responses.text: No you cannot add cards to a closed meld.

responses.author: Fran Karliner
responses.text: You cannot add to a meld of dead cards...because they are dead! It's really common sense: you add to a meld to try to make a canasta - no canasta of dead cards!

responses.author: Linda Feinstein Block
responses.text: Cards are dead

responses.author: Angela Cerina
responses.text: We play dead is dead

responses.author: Rae Glaser
responses.text: Not dead till it is closed.

responses.author: Jody Partovich Schwartz
responses.text: No

responses.author: Janet Levine
responses.text: Definitely a table rule

recommendation: Once one team closes a canasta of a particular rank, that card is DEAD for the opposing team. The opposing team CANNOT add more cards of that rank to their own incomplete meld — even though they melded it before it was closed. The card becomes a 'safe discard' since no one can use it, BUT you still cannot discard a dead card into an EMPTY discard tray (empty tray discard restrictions still apply). This is NOT a table rule — it's the standard MAC rule. One important timing nuance: cards are only dead AFTER the canasta is physically closed, not before.

author: Sandy Wachs Friedman
question: Disagreement at our game… initial meld and need 180. Can I meld with six 2's and a big joker?
context: Player has seven wild cards (six 2s and a Joker) and wants to use them as an initial meld at the 180-point threshold. Some opponents say you also need a clean triple alongside. 103 comments with major debate.

responses.author: Ilana Etzman Kopinsky
responses.text: Yes, its a canasta worth 2000 points. You couldn't do it with anything other than wild cards

responses.author: Robin Levine
responses.text: Yes you absolutely can meld on open with a wild card Canasta. That is worth a full 2000 points and it's a complete Canasta and does not warrant a clean triple. People who tell you that you can't close a wild card Canasta on open play that way as their table rule. Every group I've played with allows it.

responses.author: Doris Erlich Albirt
responses.text: Concealed canasta is a meld regardless of points. Seven coordinate is automatic out. Seven wild of any assortment is a winner

responses.author: Ellen Eisenberg Racioppi
responses.text: That is only 170 points. You would have to add a clean triple to the meld.

responses.author: Betty Dwyer Holwitt
responses.text: Yes you can-it's a canasta worth 2000 pts.

responses.author: Patti Heleva
responses.text: Yes. From the Ultimate Guide to Modern Canasta...page 32 'Every natural canasta is considered a valid initial meld, INCLUDING all the wild card canastas, even if using a mix of wild cards.'

responses.author: Carol Nachenberg Shames
responses.text: Yes! It's a wild card canasta worth 2500 points.

responses.author: Sherry Isaacson Gottlieb
responses.text: House rules seem to vary and should be determined before starting a game

responses.author: Sterra Himelstein
responses.text: It's a joker canasta. Different scoring for all 2s. Containing all 4 jokers and also for a mixture. Either way it counts as a special canasta. Don't need a clean three.

responses.author: Maxine Taglia
responses.text: yes- you can meld with 7 4's and that would have been a meld for 180

analysis.key_debate: Whether a wild card canasta counts as a valid initial meld without needing a separate clean triple

recommendation: YES, you CAN meld with seven wild cards (e.g., six 2s and a Joker) as your initial meld, even at the 180-point threshold. A completed wild card canasta IS a valid initial meld on its own — you do NOT need a separate clean triple alongside it. The wild card canasta is worth 2,000 points (mixed wilds), 2,500 (four big jokers), or 3,000 (all 2s), which far exceeds any meld point requirement. The 'Ultimate Guide to Modern Canasta' (page 32) explicitly states: 'Every natural canasta is considered a valid initial meld, INCLUDING all the wild card canastas.' Some players confuse this because they calculate card point values (six 2s = 120 + one Joker = 50 = 170) rather than recognizing that a completed canasta satisfies any meld requirement regardless of individual card points.
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: Despite 103 comments with debate, the book citation settles it definitively. Wild card canasta = valid initial meld.

author: Roy Anderson
question: The discard pile is frozen. A player already has a meld on the table (3 sevens). They keep a pair of 7s in their hand and the player before them discards a 7. Is it legal to pick up the discard pile? Can they add the 7s to the existing meld or do they have to make a new meld?
context: Question about picking up a frozen discard pile when you already have an existing meld of that rank on the table. 41 comments with a secondary debate about whether freezing the pile is even still played.

responses.author: Robin Krant
responses.text: No one plays with freezing the pack anymore. Outdated

responses.author: Christine Allan
responses.text: They can pick up the 7 if it's the card after the frozen part. Then they would lay the two 7s they have in their hand with the discard 7 as a new meld...

analysis.key_insight: Modern American Canasta has largely abandoned the 'frozen pile' concept, but groups playing classic/traditional rules still use it

recommendation: The 'frozen discard pile' is an OUTDATED concept in Modern American Canasta (MAC). Most MAC players and groups no longer use it — the MAC admin herself (Robin Krant) states 'No one plays with freezing the pack anymore.' If you ARE playing with frozen pile rules (classic Canasta or a table rule): when the pile is frozen and you want to pick it up, you must have a NATURAL PAIR in your hand matching the top card. You lay those as a NEW meld — you cannot use an existing meld on the table to justify picking the frozen pile. RECOMMENDATION: If playing MAC, don't use frozen pile rules. If playing classic/traditional Canasta, establish frozen pile rules clearly before the game.
complexity_note: Less-complexity applied: The question itself applies to a deprecated rule. The most useful takeaway is that MAC doesn't use frozen piles.
